result:
The first thing that arrives is the smell of rain on hot asphalt, a scent that is somehow both sterile and alive, like a hospital corridor suddenly invaded by a meadow. I am eight years old again, barefoot on the porch, believing the hissing street is whispering my name. I answer out loud, “I’m here,” and the echo comes back changed: I’m nowhere. The word nowhere is a small black bird that lands on the power line and refuses to leave, even when the sun goes down and the line becomes a tightrope between two dark continents.

I once tried to measure the distance between continents using a spoon. I scooped up a bit of ocean, let it evaporate, weighed the salt. I told my mother I had captured the Pacific. She said, “Don’t forget to rinse the spoon.” Mothers are the original cartographers; they redraw the world every time they wipe the counter, making entire archipelagos disappear with a floral-print cloth. I kept the salt in a matchbox. Years later I opened it and found only a small white scar, shaped exactly like my thumb. I licked it. It tasted of iron, of homework I never turned in, of the pause my father left the day he went for milk and returned as an envelope of silence.

Silence has weight. You can pile it in corners like folded laundry. When the pile gets high enough, the room begins to breathe backwards—inhaling your voice, exhaling a colder version of you. I once lived in such a room. I spoke to the pile: “Tell me something true.” The silence shifted, revealed a single red sock. I put it on my hand like a puppet. The sock said, “Truth is what’s left when the mouth forgets how to ask.” Then it bit me. I still have the mark, a crescent of tiny holes printed on the webbing between thumb and finger. It looks like a constellation no culture has ever bothered to name. I call it The Incomplete Instructions.

We are all following incomplete instructions. Mine arrived in a cereal box, wedged between the raisins and the promise of a free decoder ring. Step one: “Breathe.” Step two was missing. I spent years improvising—breathing in colors, exhaling prime numbers. At thirteen I tried to exhale 97 and fainted. When I woke, the ceiling was a fresco of every face I had disappointed. They were beautiful, like stained glass lit from behind by a fire I couldn’t see. I apologized to each shard. They apologized back, a choir of small cuts.

Time is not a line; it is a pantry. On the top shelf, the future is canned in brine, label written in a language that keeps changing. Yesterday I opened a tin dated 2037 and found only a smaller tin inside. I hear the rattling of something microscopic pacing itself to death. I put both tins in the donation bag along with the single red sock. Someone, somewhere, will know how to complete the recipe.

I keep misplacing my death. I set it down for a moment—maybe on the bus seat beside me, maybe in the comment section of a video about seabirds—and when I reach for it again, it’s gone. I panic, then feel relief, then panic at the relief. I file a report with the lost-and-found of the self. The clerk behind the counter looks exactly like me but older, wearing the same scar between thumb and finger. She says, “Are you sure you brought it in?” I describe it: silver, the size of a lentil, humming slightly. She opens a drawer. Inside are thousands of identical lentils, all humming in discordant keys. “Pick one,” she offers. “They all fit.” I ask if any of them come with instructions. She laughs, the sound of a refrigerator light dying. “They’re all step two,” she says.

So I leave without choosing. The street outside is the same one from childhood, but the porch is gone, replaced by a vending machine that sells clouds. I insert the memory of my mother wiping the counter. Out drops a small cumulus, lukewarm, nutrition facts printed on the side: 100% impossible return. I bite it. It tastes like the moment before saying goodbye, like cartoon reruns dubbed in a language you almost understand. I eat the whole thing and grow three inches of longing. The longing is directional—it points northeast. I follow.

Northeast takes me through neighborhoods that exist only between two heartbeats. The houses have windows but no glass; inside, mannequins rehearse future arguments, gesturing with plastic arms that never quite touch. One of them looks like you. You are wearing the argument we haven’t had yet about whether mercy is a kind of gravity. I wave. You wave back, but your hand falls off and rolls into the gutter, where it becomes a white mushroom. I pick it, pocket it, forget it instantly.

At the city limit, the road becomes a sentence that refuses to end. Commas multiply like rabbits; semicolons erect tollbooths. I pay with the salt scar on my thumb. The clerk—a different me, gender unspecified—stamps my passport with the word Eventually. I ask for a map. They hand me a mirror cracked into exactly 144 pieces. “Each shard shows you one step ahead,” they explain. “But only if you look sideways.” I try. I see a version of me watering a plant that grows into a library. Birds made of footnotes nest in the rafters. I check the time; my watch is melting, but politely, like a guest who knows he’s overstayed. I tuck the mirror into the same pocket as the mushroom, which has now become a tiny letter. I feel it breathing.

Night arrives wearing the scent of photocopies. I lie down in the margin of a field that belongs to no book. Above me, the constellations are rehearsing new myths. The Incomplete Instructions has been joined by other formations: The Unmailed Apology, The Miscounted Heartbeats, The Recipe for Forgiveness That Requires No Ingredients. I name them quietly, so as not to scare them off. A small black bird lands on my chest. It smells of hot asphalt and tomorrow’s rain. It opens its beak, reveals a single silver lentil. I understand: this is not my death, only its rehearsal. I swallow it anyway. It tastes like the pause before laughter, like the word someday unhooked from the calendar. I wait for the humming to begin. Instead, I hear the sound of a spoon rinsing under warm water, my mother humming off-key. The sound is so gentle it turns the night inside out, revealing a lining stitched from every morning I thought had been lost. I step inside. The porch is there, barefoot. The street whispers, “I’m here.” This time the echo answers, “I know.”

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
